content.problemStatement:
Due to a shortage of ATMs, a bank decided to convert some arcade gaming machines into ATMs. Unfortunately, due to a bug, the converted machine will only dispense cash if the withdrawer can win the game. The game is played over a valid bracket sequence. Formally, a valid bracket sequence is defined as:


An empty string is valid.
If `A` is a valid bracket sequence, then `(A)` is also valid.
If `A` and `B` are valid, `AB` is also valid.
In each move, a player can erase a non-empty valid substring of the remaining brackets such that it cannot be formed by concatenating two valid sequences. For example, a player can erase `(())` or `(()())` but not `()()` or `(())()`. The ATM always plays first. The player that is not able to make a move will lose the game. Assume that both players play optimally.

Bob is trying to withdraw money and is frustrated as he keeps losing against the machine. He suspects the machine is rigged and always wins the game. So, he has asked you to help him out. Given a valid bracket sequence, can you determine who wins the game?


Input Format
The first line of the input contains an integer `T` denoting the number of test cases. The description of `T` test cases follows.


Each test case consists of a single line containing the string `S` - the initial bracket sequence.



Output Format
For each test case, output a single line containing the winner - either "ATM" or "Bob" without quotes.
constraints: The sum of all lengths of S will be not more than 10^6
S will be a valid bracket sequence
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains an integer T denoting the number of test cases. Each test case consists of a single line containing the string S - the initial bracket sequence.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the winner - either "ATM" or "Bob" without quotes.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: The ATM will erase the entire sequence and win the game.
Example case 2: The ATM will again erase the entire sequence and win the game.
Example case 3: The ATM can erase either the first or the second (), Bob will erase the other one and win.

solution.approach: To determine the winner, we can use the concept of a Nim game. We can calculate the Nim sum of the lengths of all valid substrings in the bracket sequence. If the Nim sum is not zero, the ATM wins; otherwise, Bob wins.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N) — where T is the number of test cases and N is the maximum length of the bracket sequence. This is because we need to iterate over each character in the bracket sequence for each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — where N is the maximum length of the bracket sequence. This is because we need to store the bracket sequence for each test case.
